narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and middle-class family background, you've put together a thoughtful application to law school, including your decision to pursue full-time studies and an LSAT score of 28. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 71.1% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. The most significant factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of their class (average decile of 5.53). Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed substantially in the same direction, again placing you well below the average of 5.71 and signaling early challenges in keeping pace with peers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing since it's close to the dataset average of 3.21, showing some underlying academic strength. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, where stronger high school performance often aligns with success, your lower deciles in later years deviated more sharply from those patterns, amplifying the model's concern. While your LSAT score is below the average of 36.7 and your full-time status is common (over 90% of students), they played lesser roles here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income family—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, seeking tutoring or academic support to strengthen your class rankings in any remaining studies, and perhaps gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and non-academic strengths. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift the model's outlook significantly, opening doors to approval next time. You're clearly motivated, and with focused steps, you can rewrite this story.
